full_content:
# 평생건강관리센타 도면 구성(지상 1층 환기 덕트 평면도·데크·설문작성대·휴게공간)

이 커뮤니티는 ‘평생건강관리센타’를 중심으로, 건축/설계 문서에 해당하는 ‘지상 1층 환기 덕트 평면도’와 시설 내 공간 요소들(데크, 설문작성대, 휴게공간)이 공간 배치 및 기능적 역할을 통해 연결된 구조로 파악된다. 특히 지상 1층 환기 덕트 평면도는 평생건강관리센타의 공조·환기 시스템 이해에 필요한 도면으로 제시되며, 데크는 1층 평면 구성과 인접한 외부/내부 연결 및 순환 동선 보조 역할로 연결된다. 또한 설문작성대는 평생건강관리센타의 1층 평면에 포함된 구성요소로 확인되며, 휴게공간은 시설 내 부속 휴식/대기 공간으로 건물 도면 전반(배치도·단면도)과 연계되는 것으로 설명된다. 별도의 관계로 ‘지상 5층 건물 외피 전개도’에 휴게공간이 포함된 것으로 연결되나, 제공된 정보만으로는 지상 5층에서의 휴게공간 위치·운영 방식까지는 추가 확인이 필요하다.

## 평생건강관리센타의 공조·환기 설계 문서로서 ‘지상 1층 환기 덕트 평면도’가 핵심 연결고리

‘지상 1층 환기 덕트 평면도’는 평생건강관리센타의 전체 설계 및 건축 문서에 포함된 도면으로, 건물 1층의 환기 계획(공조 및 환기 시스템 구성)을 이해하는 데 필요한 자료로 정의된다. 즉, 이 도면이 단순 참고 그림이 아니라 센터의 설비/환경 측면 기능을 설명·구현하기 위한 문서로 기능한다는 점에서 커뮤니티의 중심 축이 된다. [Data: Entities (2096, 1181); Relationships (2976)]

다만 제공된 관계 정보는 ‘도면이 무엇을 나타내는지’(환기 계획, 설비 배치 및 이해에 필요한 자료)까지만 확인하며, 실제 운영 방식(예: 특정 구역의 환기량, 유지보수 절차)이나 규정 준수 여부를 판단할 추가 데이터는 포함되어 있지 않다. 따라서 기술적 세부 위험/규정 준수 수준 평가는 제한적이다. [Data: Entities (2096); Relationships (2976)]

## 데크는 센터의 실내-외부 연결 및 순환 동선을 보조하는 공간요소로 기능

‘데크’는 평생건강관리센타의 1층 평면과 인접한 외부 데크 공간으로, 첫 번째 층 실내 공간과 직접 이어지는 형태로 계획되어 실내-외부를 자연스럽게 연결하는 역할을 한다고 설명된다. 관계로도 ‘평생건강관리센타’가 데크를 포함한 평면 구성을 가진다고 명시되어, 데크가 단일 부속이 아니라 센터 공간 계획의 일부(이동 동선/순환 공간 역할)로 편성됨을 보여준다. [Data: Entities (1181); Relationships (1394)]

이 연결성은 이용자 동선의 연속성을 높일 수 있는 반면, 출입 흐름이나 안전 관리(예: 외부 구역과 내부 구역 간 통제, 동선 혼잡 관리) 등 운영 상의 쟁점이 될 가능성도 있다. 하지만 현재 데이터에는 출입 통제 방식, 안전 기준 충족 여부 등 ‘운영 리스크’를 직접 뒷받침하는 정보가 제공되지 않아, 영향 평가는 ‘잠재적 영향 가능성’ 수준으로만 제한된다. [Data: Entities (1181); Relationships (1394)]

## 설문작성대는 평생건강관리센타 1층 평면 구성 요소로 포함

‘설문작성대’는 1층 평면도에 표시되는 ‘질문지(설문) 작성용 책상/공간’으로 정의되어 있으며, 관계로는 평생건강관리센타의 1층 평면 일부라고 연결된다. 즉, 설문작성대는 단순 가구가 아니라 이용자 활동(설문 작성)과 관련된 공간 구성요소로서 센터 운영 프로세스의 한 부분을 드러낸다. [Data: Entities (1905); Relationships (2628)]

이와 같은 대면/절차 기반 활동이 포함된 공간은 이용자 흐름, 대기 또는 안내 동선과 연동될 수 있어 운영 리스크(예: 혼잡, 사적 정보 취급 절차 등)가 존재할 수 있으나, 제공된 데이터에는 설문 방식(종이/전자), 개인정보 처리 관행, 동선 설계 세부 등이 없다. 따라서 현재 정보만으로는 규정 준수나 보안 수준을 단정할 수 없다. [Data: Entities (1905); Relationships (2628)]

## 휴게공간은 센터 내 ‘휴식 및 대기’ 기능의 핵심 부속공간으로 정의

‘휴게공간’은 센터 건물의 일부로 포함된 휴식 및 대기 공간이며, 배치도와 건물 단면도에 모두 나타나는 공간으로 설명된다. 또한 1층 평면도에서 ‘이름 있는 공간’으로 표시되는 라운지형 휴게 구역이며, 이용자들이 잠시 쉬거나 머무를 수 있도록 설계된 부속공간이라고 명시된다. [Data: Entities (1329)]

관계 정보에서는 휴게공간이 ‘지상5층 건물 외피 전개도’에 포함된 것으로도 연결되어 있어, 휴게공간이 특정 층에만 국한된 구성인지, 혹은 여러 층에 걸친 유사 공간(또는 전개도 상의 표기)인지에 대한 추가 해석이 필요하다. 다만 현재 데이터는 ‘휴게공간’이라는 명칭의 도면상 포함/표시에 대한 연결만 제공하므로, 실제 운영 위치·규모·인원 수용 등은 판단 불가다. [Data: Entities (1329); Relationships (2369)]

## 커뮤니티는 ‘설계 문서(도면)’와 ‘공간 요소(데크·설문작성대·휴게공간)’의 관계망으로 구성

제공된 엔티티와 관계를 보면, ‘지상 1층 환기 덕트 평면도’는 설비 설계 문서로서 평생건강관리센타의 기술적 구성을 이해시키는 역할을 하며, ‘데크’, ‘설문작성대’, ‘휴게공간’은 이용자 경험과 공간 기능을 구성하는 요소로 연결된다. 즉, 이 커뮤니티는 단일 건물 자체(평생건강관리센타)를 중심으로 도면과 공간 기능이 교차 연결되는 형태이며, 기술(환기/공조)과 운영(설문 작성, 휴식/대기)의 관점이 함께 등장한다. [Data: Entities (1181, 1329, 1905, 2096); Relationships (2976, 1394, 2628, 2369)]

이 구조는 의사결정자가 건축·설비 설계 문서와 실제 공간 구성 요소를 동시에 파악하게 해, 잠재적 운영 영향(동선/체류/이용 행위)과 기술적 영향(환경 설계)을 함께 고려할 수 있게 한다. 다만, 제공 데이터는 ‘무엇이 포함되어 있다’는 수준의 설명이 중심이라, 규정 준수(예: 관련 법규/기준 충족), 보안/개인정보 보호, 안전성 검증 같은 실증 정보가 포함되어 있지 않다. [Data: Relationships (2976, 1394, 2628, 2369)]

## 관계 데이터는 ‘포함’ 중심으로, 리스크(사고·위반·취약점)를 직접 시사하는 증거는 제한적

관계 설명은 대체로 ‘도면에 포함된다’, ‘평면 구성의 일부다’, ‘표시된다’와 같은 구조적 포함 관계를 제시한다. 예를 들어 환기 덕트 평면도는 센터 설계 문서의 일부이며 환기 계획 이해에 필요한 자료로 정의되고, 데크와 설문작성대는 센터 평면 구성요소로 연결된다. 휴게공간 역시 배치도/단면도에 나타나는 공간이며 특정 외피 전개도에 포함된 것으로 연결된다. [Data: Relationships (2976, 1394, 2628, 2369)]

따라서 현 시점에서는 위반, 사고, 악용, 위험 행동 등 ‘부정적 영향’을 직접 뒷받침할 청구(Claims)나 구체적 증거가 제시되지 않는다. 결과적으로 커뮤니티의 영향 평가는 ‘운영 환경에 영향을 줄 수 있는 공간/설비가 존재함’ 정도의 잠재성만 반영해야 하며, 높은 확신의 부정적 결론은 데이터 공백으로 인해 도출하기 어렵다. [Data: Entities (2096, 1181, 1905, 1329); Relationships (2976, 1394, 2628, 2369)]

title: 평생건강관리센타(로비·접수대·검진·환기/난방 배관) 커뮤니티
summary: 이 커뮤니티는 ‘평생건강관리센타’를 중심으로 접수대, 영역별상담, 신체계측, 기초검진, 체력측정, 편의공간(계단형 북카페), 관리공간(실장실) 등 다양한 실·프로그램이 기능적으로 연결되어 있는 건물 계획/도면 기반 구조로 구성됩니다. 또한 지하 1층 환기 덕트 평면도 및 지상 1·2층 냉·난방 배관 평면도, 그리고 서비스 코어(C.P.), 창고/탕비실, 무빙 월, 엘리베이터(15인승) 같은 설비·구조 요소가 평생건강관리센타의 배치에 포함되어 운영 기능을 뒷받침하는 것으로 나타납니다. 보안 측면에서는 로비 구역에 상부 방범셔터 설치 메모가 연결되어 있고, 로비와 카페테리아가 단면도에 함께 기재되는 정황이 제시됩니다. [Data: Entities (1414,1179,2092,1902,1903,2280,2626,2634,2627,3323); Relationships (2975,1389,1390,1393,2602,2603,1395,1391,2623,2624,2626,2634,2627,3317,3323,+more)]
full_content:
# 평생건강관리센타(로비·접수대·검진·환기/난방 배관) 커뮤니티

이 커뮤니티는 ‘평생건강관리센타’를 중심으로 접수대, 영역별상담, 신체계측, 기초검진, 체력측정, 편의공간(계단형 북카페), 관리공간(실장실) 등 다양한 실·프로그램이 기능적으로 연결되어 있는 건물 계획/도면 기반 구조로 구성됩니다. 또한 지하 1층 환기 덕트 평면도 및 지상 1·2층 냉·난방 배관 평면도, 그리고 서비스 코어(C.P.), 창고/탕비실, 무빙 월, 엘리베이터(15인승) 같은 설비·구조 요소가 평생건강관리센타의 배치에 포함되어 운영 기능을 뒷받침하는 것으로 나타납니다. 보안 측면에서는 로비 구역에 상부 방범셔터 설치 메모가 연결되어 있고, 로비와 카페테리아가 단면도에 함께 기재되는 정황이 제시됩니다. [Data: Entities (1414,1179,2092,1902,1903,2280,2626,2634,2627,3323); Relationships (2975,1389,1390,1393,2602,2603,1395,1391,2623,2624,2626,2634,2627,3317,3323,+more)]

## 평생건강관리센타가 다수의 진료·상담·측정 기능 공간을 포괄하는 중심 엔티티로 연결됨

‘평생건강관리센타’는 접수대, 영역별상담, 신체계측, 기초검진, 체력측정, 계단형 북카페, 실장실 등 여러 실/기능이 서로 연결되어 운영 흐름을 형성하는 중심 공간으로 설명됩니다. 특히 도면/배치도에서 센타의 레이아웃 일부로 반복·지정되는 기능들이 관찰되어, 방문자 응대(접수/상담)부터 측정·검사(신체계측/기초검진/체력측정)까지 일련의 서비스 구성이 도면상으로 구조화되어 있음을 시사합니다. [Data: Entities (1414,1177,1180,1892,1891,1395,1178); Relationships (1390,1393,2602,2603,1389,1395,+more)]

또한 센타 내 세부 공간들은 “센터 프로그램의 일부”, “배치도에 포함”, “층별 배치와 전체 레이아웃의 일부”처럼 기능적 통합의 표현으로 근거가 제시되어, 단일 시설이 아니라 다기능 운영 단위로 계획되어 있음을 뒷받침합니다. 이는 정보공유/운영 방식이 특정 구역(접수·측정·검사 등)에 집중될 가능성을 의미하며, 시설 변경·장비 배치·동선 관리가 전반 서비스 품질에 영향을 줄 수 있습니다. [Data: Relationships (1389,1390,1393,2602,2603,+more)]

## 지하 1층 환기 덕트 평면도가 평생건강관리센타의 설계 문서 체계에 포함됨

지하 1층 환기 덕트 평면도는 ‘평생건강관리센타’의 전체 설계 문서 구성 요소로 연결되어, 센타가 위치한 건물의 공조 설계(환기 경로·장비 구성)가 시설 설계 단계에서 핵심적으로 반영되었음을 보여줍니다. 해당 도면 설명에는 지하 주차장, 기계실/유틸리티실, 저수조, 펌프실, 전기실, 집수정(sump pit) 등 지하 구역과 환기 덕트 라우팅 및 공기 흐름 요소가 종합적으로 표현된다고 되어 있어, 단순 평면이 아니라 설비 간 간섭 저감 및 시공/유지관리 기준 자료 역할을 목표로 하는 문서 성격이 제시됩니다. [Data: Entities (2092); Relationships (2975)]

이 연결은 센타 운영과 직접 연동되는 공조·환기 요구사항(예: 이용자 체류 공간, 검사/측정 공간 등)의 만족 가능성을 설계상 고려했을 가능성을 의미하지만, 구체적인 공조 성능 수치나 위생 기준 충족 여부는 제공된 자료에서 확인되지 않습니다. 다만 설비 설계 문서가 센타 단위로 묶여 있다는 점에서, 센타 변경/증설 시 환기 설계 검토가 필요할 수 있는 구조적 의존성이 존재함을 시사합니다. [Data: Entities (2092); Relationships (2975)]

## 1·2층 냉·난방 배관 평면도가 센타의 공간 범위를 실제로 포함하는 설비 문서로 연결됨

‘지상 1층 냉,난방 배관 평면도’ 및 ‘지상 2층 냉,난방 배관 평면도’는 각각 평생건강관리센타의 설계 문서(배치/공간 묘사)로 연결되며, 센타 내부 공간이 난방·냉방 배관 계획과 함께 도시되어 있음을 나타냅니다. 도면 설명은 해당 배관 평면도가 층별 냉·난방 배치(구역, 배관 유형, 치수, 장비 위치)를 보여주는 문서임을 명시합니다. [Data: Entities (1902,1903); Relationships (2623,2624)]

이러한 연결은 센타의 이용 공간과 설비 공간이 단순 배치가 아니라 배관 네트워크를 고려한 통합 설계로 계획되었을 가능성을 높입니다. 결과적으로 센타 공간 용도(예: 검사실, 상담/측정 구역) 변경이나 장비 추가가 생기면 배관 동선·장비 위치·치수 재검토가 요구될 수 있으며, 운영 연속성 측면에서 설비 변경 비용/리스크가 발생할 수 있습니다(단, 구체 리스크 수준은 자료에 근거가 부족). [Data: Entities (1902,1903); Relationships (2623,2624)]

## 서비스 코어(C.P.)가 평생건강관리센타 계획에서 기능적 핵심 요소로 지정됨

서비스 코어(C.P.)는 평생건강관리센타 계획의 서비스/core 요소로 연결되어, 건물 내부에서 수직 동선·설비 집약·서비스 기능이 집중되는 구역 역할을 수행할 가능성을 보여줍니다. ‘C.P.’는 계획에서 라벨된 샤프트 또는 서비스 코어 요소로 나타난다고 설명되어 있습니다. [Data: Entities (2280); Relationships (3317)]

서비스 코어가 센타 계획에 포함된다는 점은 센타의 공간 구성(접수/상담/측정/검진 등)과 함께 설비·동선의 기반 인프라가 설계 단계에서 명확히 구획되었음을 의미합니다. 보안/운영 관점에서는 서비스 코어 주변으로 설비 접근 또는 내부 물류 동선이 존재할 수 있으나, 제공된 자료에는 출입통제, 접근 정책, 보안 등급 같은 구체 정보가 없습니다. 따라서 코어 존재 자체는 기술적·운영적 중요성을 시사하되, 위험도(악용 가능성 등) 판단은 추가 자료 없이는 제한됩니다. [Data: Entities (2280); Relationships (3317)]

## 로비 구역에 상부 방범셔터 설치 메모가 연결되어 보안 고려가 반영됨

‘상부 방범셔터 설치’는 로비 구역에 대한 설치 메모로 연결되어, 로비 영역에 상부 보안 셔터가 고려/기재되었음을 나타냅니다. 이는 로비가 건물의 1층 평면에서 공용 진입 및 이동 동선의 중심 공간으로 설명되는 것과 맞물려, 이용자 대면 공간에 대해 방범 요소를 별도 메모로 명시했다는 점이 중요합니다. [Data: Entities (1179,2281); Relationships (3323)]

다만 이 자료는 실제 설치가 완료되었는지, 셔터가 상시/비상/시간제 운영되는지, 외부 침입 위협 유형(절도·침입 등)에 대한 구체 근거는 제공하지 않습니다. 그럼에도 로비에 보안 장치 설치가 문서화되어 있다는 사실은 운영 안정성(야간/비운영 시간 통제 등) 관점에서 통제 체계가 존재할 가능성을 시사하며, 운영 절차와 함께 점검할 필요가 있습니다. [Data: Entities (1179,2281); Relationships (3323)]

## 엘리베이터(15인승)·무빙 월 등 이동/가변 요소가 센타 계획에 포함됨

15인승 엘리베이터는 평생건강관리센타 건물 레이아웃에 표시된 이동 수단으로 연결되며, 무빙 월은 평생건강관리센타의 2층 레이아웃에서 보이는 가변/이동식 벽체 또는 파티션 시스템으로 연결됩니다. [Data: Entities (1238,1253); Relationships (2627,2634)]

가변 요소(무빙 월)와 수용/이동 설비(15인승)가 존재한다는 점은 센타 공간이 고정형이 아니라 운영 목적에 따라 일부 레이아웃이 조정될 수 있음을 의미할 수 있습니다. 이는 프로그램 운영(상담 구역/검사 구역/대기 공간 구성 등)의 유연성을 제공할 가능성이 있지만, 동시에 동선·실 분리·설비 간섭(냉난방/환기/전기/방범 등)의 재검토가 필요해질 수 있습니다. 다만 구체 재배치 방식이나 실제 운영 시나리오는 제공된 근거에서 확인되지 않습니다. [Data: Entities (1253,1238); Relationships (2634,2627)]

## 기초검진·체력측정·신체계측 등 검사/평가 기능이 센타 내 핵심 공간으로 배치됨

평생건강관리센타에는 기초검진, 체력측정, 신체계측 공간이 포함되어 있으며, 각각 이용자의 건강 상태를 확인/측정하기 위한 ‘기본 검사’ 및 ‘물리적 측정/평가’ 기능으로 설명됩니다. 이들은 센타의 층별 배치 및 전체 레이아웃의 일부로 연결되어, 센타가 단순 상담 중심 시설이 아니라 측정·검사 중심 서비스 흐름을 갖는 것으로 나타납니다. [Data: Entities (1180,1892,1891); Relationships (1393,2602,2603)]

이러한 기능적 묶음은 이용자 개인정보·의료 데이터 취급 가능성(예: 검사 결과 기록 등)을 시사할 수 있으나, 제공된 자료에는 데이터 처리, 보관, 접근통제, 법적 준수 사항(의료정보 보호 관련)이 명시되어 있지 않습니다. 따라서 ‘기능이 존재한다’는 수준까지만 근거 기반으로 말할 수 있고, 실제 규제 준수 수준은 추가 자료 확인이 필요합니다. [Data: Relationships (1393,2602,2603)]

## 창고/탕비실 및 실장실 등 운영 보조·관리 공간도 센타 계획에 포함됨

평생건강관리센타에는 창고/탕비실이 포함되어 물품 보관 및 간단 준비 용도로 사용되는 별도 구역으로 설명되며, 실장실은 관리자의 사무/디렉터 공간으로 센타 계획에 포함됩니다. [Data: Entities (1904,1178); Relationships (2626,1391)]

운영 보조·관리 공간의 존재는 센타가 일상 운영(물품/준비/관리) 기능을 갖춘 복합 시설임을 보여줍니다. 다만 제공된 자료는 해당 공간의 보안 수준(접근 권한), 위생 운영 절차, 또는 관련 규정 준수(예: 위생/안전 기준) 같은 구체 내용을 포함하지 않습니다. 결론적으로, 센타 운영의 ‘기능 분업’ 가능성은 높게 시사되지만, 규정 준수나 위험성 평가는 추가 증거가 필요합니다. [Data: Relationships (2626,1391)]

## 로비와 카페테리아가 도면(단면)에서 모두 ‘실내 프로그램 공간’으로 함께 기재됨

로비와 카페테리아는 단면도(section drawing)에 함께 등재된 ‘실내 프로그램 공간’으로 연결되며, 로비는 공용 진입 및 이동 동선의 중심 역할로 설명됩니다. [Data: Entities (1179); Relationships (1804,3323)]

이 연결은 센타의 이용자 경험(대기·이동·휴식 가능성)이 로비 인근의 프로그램 공간과 통합적으로 고려되었을 수 있음을 시사합니다. 다만 카페테리아(카페테리아 엔티티 자체)의 별도 상세 설명은 관계 정보에만 간접적으로 반영되어 있어, 위생/주방 설비·인허가·운영 방식 같은 규제 준수는 본 자료만으로 특정하기 어렵습니다. 따라서 커뮤니티의 구조적 특징(대면 공용공간의 존재)까지만 근거 기반으로 해석하는 것이 적절합니다. [Data: Relationships (1804)]

title: 지상 4층 위생 배관 도면 및 관련 실(접수/검진/휴게/창고) 커뮤니티
summary: 이 커뮤니티는 ‘지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도’를 중심으로, 같은 4층 공간들(접수/기초상담실, 검진.2, 탕비 및 창고, 임산부 휴게실)과 더불어 ‘지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도’ 및 ‘지상 4층 확대 위생 배관 평면도’ 같은 도면 자산을 서로 연결하는 구조로 구성됩니다. 특히 지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도는 옥상휴게 마당을 포함하는 것으로 연결되어 있으며, 확대 위생 배관 평면도는 접합·연결 및 상세를 위한 참조 도면으로 기능하는 것으로 설명됩니다. 동시에 여러 실(접수/기초상담실, 검진.2, 탕비 및 창고, 임산부 휴게실)은 각각 지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도에 ‘라벨링된 공간’으로 연결되어 있어, 위생 배관/환기 덕트 도면 체계가 함께 사용되는 설비 계획·시공 맥락이 드러납니다. [Data: Entities (2040,2046,1217,1220,2041,2045); Relationships (2875,2902,3102,3105,3103,+more)]
full_content:
# 지상 4층 위생 배관 도면 및 관련 실(접수/검진/휴게/창고) 커뮤니티

이 커뮤니티는 ‘지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도’를 중심으로, 같은 4층 공간들(접수/기초상담실, 검진.2, 탕비 및 창고, 임산부 휴게실)과 더불어 ‘지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도’ 및 ‘지상 4층 확대 위생 배관 평면도’ 같은 도면 자산을 서로 연결하는 구조로 구성됩니다. 특히 지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도는 옥상휴게 마당을 포함하는 것으로 연결되어 있으며, 확대 위생 배관 평면도는 접합·연결 및 상세를 위한 참조 도면으로 기능하는 것으로 설명됩니다. 동시에 여러 실(접수/기초상담실, 검진.2, 탕비 및 창고, 임산부 휴게실)은 각각 지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도에 ‘라벨링된 공간’으로 연결되어 있어, 위생 배관/환기 덕트 도면 체계가 함께 사용되는 설비 계획·시공 맥락이 드러납니다. [Data: Entities (2040,2046,1217,1220,2041,2045); Relationships (2875,2902,3102,3105,3103,+more)]

## 지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도(2040)가 커뮤니티의 중심 도면 역할

지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도는 4층의 위생 배관 배치를 종합적으로 보여주는 건물 설비용 도면으로, 배관 경로와 연결 상태, 관통부 상세, 치수·단열 관련 사항 및 시공 주의사항/설치 지침이 함께 포함된 것으로 설명됩니다. 이는 커뮤니티 내에서 ‘위생 설비의 설계·검토 기준’이 되는 핵심 기준 자산임을 시사합니다. [Data: Entities (2040)]

또한 이 도면은 ‘지상 4층 확대 위생 배관 평면도(2046)’를 참조하는 관계로 연결되어, 기본 평면도에서 상세(연결·디테일)로 내려가는 체계가 있음을 보여줍니다. 즉, 2040은 전체 배치 개요를 제공하고 2046은 연결/상세 구현을 위한 확장 정보를 제공하는 역할 분담 구조입니다. [Data: Relationships (2902); Entities (2046)]

## 확대 위생 배관 평면도(2046)는 연결·상세 구현을 위한 참조 도면으로 기능

지상 4층 확대 위생 배관 평면도(2046)는 4층 위생 배관 계획을 “연결 및 디테일을 위해” 사용하는 확대 참조 도면이라고 설명되어 있습니다. 따라서 커뮤니티 내에서 2046은 실제 시공 단계에서 필요한 접합/연결 세부를 확인하기 위한 보조적이면서도 구체적인 기준 자료로 자리합니다. [Data: Entities (2046)]

또한 2040(지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도)가 2046을 참조한다고 명시되어 있어, 사용자(시공자·검토자)가 기본 배치도→확대 상세도 순으로 검증 및 시공을 진행할 가능성이 높습니다. 이러한 도면 연결은 설비 설치 시 오류 가능성을 줄이고, 부품/연결부의 정확한 구현을 돕는 구조적 특징으로 해석됩니다. [Data: Relationships (2902)]

## 옥상휴게 마당과의 연결(2875)은 위생 배관 계획이 외부/공용 공간 요소를 포함할 가능성 시사

관계 데이터에 따르면 지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도는 ‘옥상휴게 마당’을 포함하는 것으로 설명됩니다. 이는 위생 배관이 단일 실 내부뿐 아니라 옥상 휴게/마당 영역 같은 공용 또는 준외부 구역과도 연동되어 계획되었음을 나타낼 수 있습니다. [Data: Relationships (2875)]

다만 제공된 데이터에서는 옥상휴게 마당이 어떤 방식으로(예: 배수, 급수, 관통부 처리 등) 연동되는지는 구체화되어 있지 않습니다. 그럼에도 “포함(includes)” 관계가 존재한다는 사실 자체가, 설비 범위가 건물 내부 특정 실에만 한정되지 않을 수 있음을 보여주는 정황입니다. [Data: Relationships (2875)]

## 접수/기초상담실(1217)은 환기 덕트 평면도(3102)에 라벨된 공간으로 연결

접수/기초상담실(1217)은 방문 접수 및 초기 상담을 수행하는 공간으로 정의되어 있습니다. 관계 데이터는 이 공간이 지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도(3102)의 ‘라벨링된 공간’ 중 하나라고 설명합니다. [Data: Entities (1217); Relationships (3102)]

따라서 커뮤니티는 위생 배관(2040/2046) 중심뿐 아니라, 환기 덕트 설계에서도 특정 기능 공간(접수/상담)의 설비 계획이 함께 표기되는 체계를 포함합니다. 환기 설비가 사용자 동선 및 체류 시간과 맞물릴 수 있는 점을 고려하면, 이 공간이 설비 계획 문서에 연결되어 있다는 사실은 운영 환경(공기질/환기)과 관련된 잠재적 영향이 있음을 시사합니다. [Data: Relationships (3102)]

## 검진.2(1220)는 환기 덕트 평면도(3105)의 라벨된 진료/검사 공간으로 연결

검진.2(1220)는 건강 검진용 진료/검사 공간의 하나로 설명됩니다. 관계 데이터는 검진.2가 지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도(3105) 상의 라벨된 방 중 하나라고 명시합니다. [Data: Entities (1220); Relationships (3105)]

이 연결은 검진 기능이 수행되는 공간이 환기 덕트 계획 문서에 반영되어 있음을 의미합니다. 제공된 데이터는 덕트 규모나 필터링 여부, 환기 방식 같은 구체 성능 정보는 포함하지 않지만, 적어도 도면 수준에서 ‘환기 설비-기능 공간’의 매칭이 이루어지고 있음을 보여줍니다. [Data: Relationships (3105)]

## 탕비 및 창고(2041)는 환기 덕트 평면도(3103)의 라벨된 공간으로 연결

탕비 및 창고(2041)는 4층 평면도에 표시된 공간으로 탕비 기능과 함께 물품 보관(창고) 역할을 하는 구역으로 설명됩니다. 관계 데이터에 따르면 이 공간은 지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도(3103)에서 라벨된 공간 중 하나입니다. [Data: Entities (2041); Relationships (3103)]

이처럼 탕비·보관 성격의 공간이 환기 덕트 도면에 연결되어 있다는 점은, 물품 보관 환경(환기 필요성) 또는 탕비 관련 작업 구역의 공기 관리가 설비 계획에 포함되었을 가능성을 보여줍니다. 다만 데이터는 연결 방식이나 환기 요구 수준을 직접적으로 제공하지는 않습니다. [Data: Relationships (3103)]

## 임산부 휴게실(2045)은 환기 덕트 평면도(3109)의 라벨된 공간으로 연결

임산부 휴게실(2045)은 임산부 전용 휴게 공간으로 설계되어 있으며 4층 평면도에서 확인된다고 설명됩니다. 관계 데이터는 임산부 휴게실이 지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도(3109)의 라벨된 공간임을 나타냅니다. [Data: Entities (2045); Relationships (3109)]

임산부 전용 휴게 공간은 상대적으로 취약할 수 있는 이용자 특성(휴식/체류)이 있을 수 있으므로, 환기 설비 계획 문서에 연결되어 있다는 사실 자체가 운영 환경의 중요성을 시사합니다. 다만 제공된 증거는 환기 성능(예: ACH, 압력구배, 필터 등)을 제시하지 않으므로 구체적인 안전/보건 판단은 추가 데이터가 필요합니다. [Data: Relationships (3109)]

## 법적/평판 리스크에 대한 직접 단서 부재: 제공 데이터는 ‘설비 도면/공간’ 중심

제공된 엔터티와 관계는 모두 건물 설비 도면(지상 4층 위생 배관 평면도, 확대 위생 배관 평면도, 환기 덕트 평면도)과 기능 공간(접수/기초상담실, 검진.2, 탕비 및 창고, 임산부 휴게실)으로 구성되어 있습니다. 어떤 엔터티도 법적 분쟁, 규제 위반, 보안 사고, 또는 평판 이슈에 대한 ‘청구/주장(Claims)’이나 관련 증거가 제공되지 않았습니다. [Data: Entities (2040,2046,1217,1220,2041,2045); Relationships (+more)]

따라서 이 커뮤니티의 ‘임팩트(impact) 심각도’는 주로 설비 설계/시공 정확성과 관련된 운영·안전 영향 가능성 정도로 제한적으로 해석되며, 사회적 위협이나 범죄/악의적 활동 같은 고위험 범주는 데이터가 뒷받침하지 않습니다. [Data: Entities (2040,2046,1217,1220,2041,2045)]

# 4th-Floor Ventilation Duct Plan Linking Rooftop Yard and Exam Rooms (검진.1~검진.5)

This community is structured around a single architectural/engineering floor plan entity—"지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도"—which serves as the hub connecting multiple labeled medical examination rooms (검진.1, 검진.3, 검진.4, 검진.5) and a rooftop resting yard (옥상휴게 마당). The related entities are not described as organizations or people, but as mapped spaces: the ventilation duct plan provides the layout context in which each exam room and the rooftop yard appear as labeled features on the fourth-floor drawing. The provided dataset supports that Exam 1, Exam 3, Exam 4, and Exam 5 are specifically identified as labeled rooms on that plan, and that the rooftop resting yard is also included among the spaces shown. 

## "지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도" is the central hub that links multiple facility spaces.

The community’s structure is centered on the ventilation duct floor plan drawing for the 4th floor ("지상 4층 환기 덕트 평면도"), which is explicitly described as showing ventilation-related installation details such as duct layout and diffuser schedule. Multiple other entities are connected to this single plan through relationships that indicate those spaces are labeled on (or included within) the same fourth-floor drawing context.

This hub-and-spoke relationship suggests that the plan is the primary reference artifact for understanding how different rooms and areas coexist on the fourth floor, at least from the perspective of ventilation layout and labeling. [Data: Entities (2155); Relationships (3100, 3104, 3106, 3107, 3108)]

## Rooftop resting yard (옥상휴게 마당) is included as a space shown on the fourth-floor ventilation duct plan.

The rooftop resting yard ("옥상휴게 마당") is connected to the ventilation duct plan via a relationship stating that the rooftop resting yard is one of the spaces shown on the 4th-floor ventilation duct plan. This places the rooftop yard within the same documented layout scope as the ventilation system elements and other labeled areas.

Because the only grounded information provided ties the rooftop yard to being “one of the spaces shown” on the plan (rather than describing its specific ventilation interaction), the most defensible conclusion is that it is spatially co-referenced with ventilation-related drawings. [Data: Relationships (3100)]

## Exam room 검진.3 is labeled as an examination room (Exam 3) on the fourth-floor ventilation duct plan.

The entity "검진.3" is described as part of health checkup/treatment and inspection spaces. Additionally, the relationship from "검진.3" to the ventilation duct plan states that "Exam 3" is one of the labeled rooms on the 4th-floor plan.

Taken together, the grounded evidence supports that the facility’s exam rooms are mapped onto the same drawing artifact used for ventilation planning, at least in terms of room identification and labeling on the floor plan. [Data: Entities (1221); Relationships (3106)]

## Exam room 검진.4 is labeled as an examination room (Exam 4) on the fourth-floor ventilation duct plan.

The entity "검진.4" is described as part of health checkup/treatment and inspection spaces. The relationship to the ventilation duct plan further specifies that "Exam 4" is one of the labeled rooms on the 4th-floor plan.

This establishes that multiple exam-room designations (beyond a single room) are present on the ventilation duct floor plan, implying a recurring mapping pattern of clinical spaces within the ventilation drawing scope. [Data: Entities (1222); Relationships (3107)]

## Exam room 검진.1 is labeled as an examination room (Exam 1) on the fourth-floor ventilation duct plan.

The entity "검진.1" is described as part of health checkup/treatment and inspection spaces. The relationship to the ventilation duct plan states that "Exam 1" is one of the labeled rooms on the 4th-floor plan.

While the descriptions do not provide explicit technical details linking ventilation performance to this particular room, the grounded relationship confirms that the room is identified as a labeled feature within the same fourth-floor ventilation duct layout document. [Data: Entities (1219); Relationships (3104)]

## Exam room 검진.5 is labeled as an examination room (Exam 5) on the fourth-floor ventilation duct plan.

The entity "검진.5" includes a more detailed description: it is a medical examination room shown on the floor plan as Exam 5, and identified in the fourth-floor plan as 검진.5; it is designated for clinical evaluation within the facility and located on the fourth floor.

The relationship additionally supports that "Exam 5" is one of the labeled rooms on the 4th-floor plan. This combination of entity-level description and relationship-level confirmation provides the strongest grounded linkage in the dataset between a named clinical space and its representation on the fourth-floor plan associated with ventilation duct layout. [Data: Entities (2044); Relationships (3108)]

## The community appears to be a documentation/mapping cluster rather than an operational network of actors.

All provided entities are architectural or spatial/documentation concepts (a ventilation duct floor plan and exam rooms and a rooftop resting yard). There are no organizations, individuals, or procedural/operational relationships described—only spatial inclusion and labeling relationships tying rooms/areas to the plan.

Accordingly, the “impact” implied by this community is limited to the informational/structural significance of having multiple clinical rooms and a rooftop yard documented within a ventilation duct plan. The dataset does not include claims about safety incidents, compliance status, or malicious intent, so no such conclusions should be inferred beyond the mapping relationships provided. [Data: Entities (2155, 1221, 1222, 1219, 2044); Relationships (3100, 3104, 3106, 3107, 3108)]

full_content:
# Basement HVAC Community: AHU-01/AHU-02/AHU-03 and FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR

This community centers on the building’s HVAC infrastructure as represented in an HVAC flow diagram. Three air handling units—AHU-01, AHU-02, and AHU-03—are each associated with the FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR, where they serve heating and cooling functions for different basement-related areas (B1 to 1st floor generally, a B1 first-floor current velocity experience area, and a marine survival experience area). The relationships indicate a shared structural linkage: each AHU is configured to supply heating and cooling to systems and spaces located within or tied to the basement-related zone represented by FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR, suggesting coordinated mechanical conditioning coverage across multiple underground areas. [Data: Entities (3247, 3263, 3248, 3249); Relationships (4626, 4627, 4628)]

## FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR is the common hub connecting all three air handling units.

FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR functions as the central “location/zone” entity in this community, referenced as the basement level within the HVAC diagram for multiple uses. All three air handling units (AHU-01, AHU-02, and AHU-03) have explicit relationships to FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR, indicating that the mechanical conditioning represented by these AHUs is tied to this underground level’s diagrammatic zone. [Data: Entities (3263, 3247, 3248, 3249); Relationships (4626, 4627, 4628)]

Because FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR is the shared target of each AHU relationship, it represents the key structural linkage in the community: regardless of which specific area is served, the heating and cooling supply responsibilities converge on the basement-related zone captured in the diagram. This makes FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR the most relevant entity for understanding the scope and coverage of the community’s HVAC function. [Data: Relationships (4626, 4627, 4628)]

## AHU-01 supports heating and cooling for the B1 to 1st floor system via FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR.

AHU-01 is identified as an air handling unit shown in the HVAC flow diagram and used for B1 to 1st floor heating and cooling supply. Its relationship to FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR explicitly states that AHU-01 serves the B1 to 1st floor heating and cooling system, with a combined relationship degree value reflecting its modeled importance in the HVAC network. [Data: Entities (3247); Relationships (4626)]

This indicates AHU-01’s role in providing conditioning capacity that spans at least the B1 and 1st floor context, while still being connected to the FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR as the diagram’s basement-related linkage point. In community impact terms, AHU-01 is a critical node because its HVAC output is described as serving a larger vertical scope (B1 to 1st floor) rather than a single named experience area. [Data: Entities (3247); Relationships (4626)]

## AHU-02 serves the B1 first-floor current velocity experience area heating and cooling system through FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR.

AHU-02 is described as an air handling unit in the HVAC flow diagram used for the B1 first-floor current velocity experience area heating and cooling supply. Its relationship to FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR specifies that AHU-02 serves the heating and cooling system for that current velocity experience area. [Data: Entities (3248); Relationships (4627)]

Compared to AHU-01, AHU-02’s description ties it more specifically to a particular experience area (current velocity experience area) rather than the broader “B1 to 1st floor” phrasing. This suggests the community includes both general vertical conditioning (AHU-01) and more targeted conditioning for specific functional spaces within the basement-related zone. [Data: Entities (3248); Relationships (4627)]

## AHU-03 is linked to the marine survival experience area and is located within the basement-related zone.

AHU-03 is identified as an air handling unit shown in the HVAC flow diagram used for the marine survival experience area heating and cooling supply. The relationship description further clarifies that AHU-03 serves the marine survival experience area heating and cooling system, which is located in the basement-related zone of the diagram. [Data: Entities (3249); Relationships (4628)]

This provides the most explicit “zone placement” context among the three AHUs: it explicitly states the marine survival experience area system is located in the basement-related zone represented by FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR. As a result, AHU-03 appears to be directly responsible for conditioning a specific basement-zone-contained experience area, making it a key node for any analysis of HVAC coverage within the underground portion of the modeled environment. [Data: Entities (3249); Relationships (4628)]

## All modeled relationships in this community point from each AHU to FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR, implying a unified basement HVAC supply structure.

In the provided relationships, the source entity is each AHU (AHU-01, AHU-02, AHU-03) and the target is consistently FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR, with each relationship documenting the respective heating/cooling system served. This consistency indicates a single structural pattern in the community’s representation: the basement level is the anchor for the HVAC supply relationships of these air handling units. [Data: Relationships (4626, 4627, 4628)]

Because every relationship targets FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR, the community’s internal structure is effectively star-like around the basement zone entity. From an impact perspective, such a structure can concentrate relevance: issues affecting the represented basement-zone interface (as captured in the diagram) could simultaneously affect multiple air handling unit functions tied to that zone. [Data: Relationships (4626, 4627, 4628)]

## The community’s scope is limited to four entities (three AHUs and one basement level) as defined by the dataset.

The dataset contains exactly four entities relevant to this community narrative: AHU-01, AHU-02, AHU-03, and FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR. There are three relationships, each connecting one AHU to FIRST BASEMENT FLOOR with descriptions indicating heating and cooling supply responsibilities. [Data: Entities (3247, 3263, 3248, 3249); Relationships (4626, 4627, 4628)]

With no additional entities (e.g., controllers, fans, duct branches, or other system components) and no claims beyond HVAC supply descriptions, the community’s characterization is necessarily constrained to mechanical-conditioning linkage as represented in the diagram. This limitation also affects potential impact assessment: the evidence supports structural HVAC connectivity, but does not provide information about operational failures, regulatory issues, or security concerns. [Data: Entities (3247, 3263, 3248, 3249); Relationships (4626, 4627, 4628)]

title: HVAC SYSTEM Thermal Network (AHU-01, ESS ROOM, Heat Exchanger)
summary: This community is a building services subsystem centered on the HVAC SYSTEM, which coordinates air and hydronic components to condition indoor spaces. Within the network, an ESS ROOM is included as part of the broader zoning/plan, and multiple thermal/hydronic elements—such as the WATER SUPPLY UNIT, HOT WATER TANK, HEAT EXCHANGER, and TRAP—are connected through explicitly defined relationships indicating how heating, heat exchange, and condensate/drain management are implemented. The relationships show a connected topology where the HVAC SYSTEM relates to air-handling (AHU-01) and overall zoning (ESS ROOM), while the heating/thermal path ties HOT WATER TANK and HEAT EXCHANGER to the WATER SUPPLY UNIT, with TRAP components present near heat exchangers in the piping schematic.
full_content:
# HVAC SYSTEM Thermal Network (AHU-01, ESS ROOM, Heat Exchanger)

This community is a building services subsystem centered on the HVAC SYSTEM, which coordinates air and hydronic components to condition indoor spaces. Within the network, an ESS ROOM is included as part of the broader zoning/plan, and multiple thermal/hydronic elements—such as the WATER SUPPLY UNIT, HOT WATER TANK, HEAT EXCHANGER, and TRAP—are connected through explicitly defined relationships indicating how heating, heat exchange, and condensate/drain management are implemented. The relationships show a connected topology where the HVAC SYSTEM relates to air-handling (AHU-01) and overall zoning (ESS ROOM), while the heating/thermal path ties HOT WATER TANK and HEAT EXCHANGER to the WATER SUPPLY UNIT, with TRAP components present near heat exchangers in the piping schematic.

## HVAC SYSTEM is the central coordinating entity linking building services components and zoning.

The HVAC SYSTEM is described as the overall heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning infrastructure that coordinates air-handling and hydronic components to distribute conditioned air and water through the facility. It includes arrangements of AHUs, HVUs, FCUs, boilers/heat pumps, and associated piping/ductwork, indicating the system supports both ventilation and thermal conditioning across spaces [Data: Entities (2972)].

Within the provided network, HVAC SYSTEM is directly connected to AHU-01 as one of its components, and it is also connected to the ESS ROOM as part of the broader building systems plan/zoning diagram. This suggests the community’s structure is organized around HVAC SYSTEM as an integration hub that ties together both terminal equipment and room-level functional spaces [Data: Relationships (4641, 4657)].

## AHU-01 represents the air-handling endpoint within the HVAC SYSTEM component set.

AHU-01 is identified as a specific component under the HVAC SYSTEM, implying it is one of the building’s air-handling units participating in the overall distribution and ventilation/conditioning workflow. The explicit relationship indicates a direct containment/association: HVAC SYSTEM → AHU-01 [Data: Relationships (4641)].

Because the HVAC SYSTEM is characterized as a coordinated network for moving conditioned air, the presence of an explicitly named AHU-01 anchors the community’s “air side” of the system architecture, complementing the “water/thermal side” elements connected via the hydronic diagram [Data: Entities (2972); Relationships (4641)].

## ESS ROOM is incorporated into the HVAC zoning/plan, indicating it is part of the broader systems layout.

The ESS ROOM is described as a basement-two room housing energy storage systems, and it is included in the HVAC zoning diagram within the broader building systems plan. The relationship HVAC SYSTEM → ESS ROOM explicitly places this room within the same overall system planning context as the HVAC infrastructure [Data: Entities (3280); Relationships (4657)].

While the provided relationships do not specify a direct thermal/hydronic coupling between the ESS ROOM and the heating/water components, its inclusion in the HVAC zoning/plan suggests co-location and potential operational interdependence at the building systems level (e.g., shared space planning, power/controls adjacency, or integrated design considerations). This is inferred only at the planning/layout level because no further technical coupling is stated in the data [Data: Relationships (4657)].

## WATER SUPPLY UNIT is the hydronic backbone that connects heat exchange and storage elements.

The WATER SUPPLY UNIT is defined as a supply unit shown in the thermal/hydronic system diagram, and it appears as a connection point for other hydronic components. Specifically, HEAT EXCHANGER is linked as part of the thermal equipment connected to the supply system, and HOT WATER TANK is linked as connected with the supply-unit hydronic equipment [Data: Entities (3257, 584, 1782); Relationships (4636, 4635)].

These relationships indicate WATER SUPPLY UNIT functions as the “backbone” for the thermal/hydronic path in this community: HOT WATER TANK → WATER SUPPLY UNIT (via the described hydronic connection), and HEAT EXCHANGER is also part of the thermal equipment connected to the supply system [Data: Relationships (4635, 4636)].

## HEAT EXCHANGER is a central repeated thermal component connected to the supply system.

The HEAT EXCHANGER is described as a functional component within the thermal system that appears multiple times in the system diagram and plays a central role in the ventilation unit’s heat exchange process. It is associated with heat exchange efficiency specifications and is highlighted as an important repeated element supporting effective ventilation and temperature regulation [Data: Entities (584)].

In the network, HEAT EXCHANGER is connected to WATER SUPPLY UNIT via the relationship HEAT EXCHANGER → WATER SUPPLY UNIT, describing that the heat exchanger is part of the thermal equipment connected to the supply system [Data: Relationships (4636)]. Taken together, the data portrays the heat exchanger as both (1) a repeated/central design element and (2) a node connected directly to the hydronic supply path, making it a key contributor to the community’s thermal performance [Data: Entities (584); Relationships (4636)].

## HOT WATER TANK provides stored hot water and is connected to the hydronic supply equipment.

The HOT WATER TANK is described as a storage-type hot water tank included in the equipment lineup, with specified dimensions and capacity, indicating it serves as a storage vessel and part of the heating system’s equipment [Data: Entities (1782)].

The relationship HOT WATER TANK → WATER SUPPLY UNIT states that the hot water tank is connected with the supply-unit hydronic equipment shown in the diagram. This indicates a direct integration between stored hot water and the supply-side hydronic distribution for the thermal system [Data: Relationships (4635)].

## TRAP elements are repeatedly present near HEAT EXCHANGER piping connections, supporting condensate/drain management.

TRAP is described as a condensate/drain trap repeatedly indicated in the piping schematic, and it is explicitly connected in the network as TRAP → HEAT EXCHANGER with a description that traps are shown repeatedly in piping connections near the heat exchangers [Data: Entities (3258); Relationships (4637)].

This relationship suggests that condensate/drain handling is an important supportive function adjacent to the heat exchange process within the community’s thermal/hydronic design. While the data does not describe failure modes or operational risk, it does establish that the trapping mechanism is repeatedly positioned in proximity to HEAT EXCHANGER components, indicating a recurring, design-significant feature of the system’s plumbing layout [Data: Entities (3258); Relationships (4637)].

## System capability emphasis is on coordinated thermal comfort delivery using both air-side integration (AHU) and hydronic heat exchange/storage (heat exchanger, tank).

At the system level, the HVAC SYSTEM description emphasizes coordination of conditioned air and water through the facility, regulating temperature and ventilation while maintaining indoor environmental quality. It also explicitly notes both VAV and CAV equipment configurations, plus heating and cooling capability implied by the presence of boilers/heat pumps (as described) and ventilation management via AHUs/HVUs/FCUs [Data: Entities (2972)].

Within the provided connections, this capability is reflected by pairing an air-side component (AHU-01) with a hydronic thermal subpath (HOT WATER TANK and HEAT EXCHANGER connected to the WATER SUPPLY UNIT, with TRAP near heat exchangers). The combination of these elements indicates the community represents a complete thermal comfort delivery chain rather than a single isolated component [Data: Relationships (4641, 4635, 4636, 4637); Entities (2972, 584, 1782, 3257, 3258)].

No legal, organizational, or reputational claims are provided; the available information focuses purely on documentation/metadata usage within technical drawings.
full_content:
# Architectural Drawing Title Block Fields: NAME OF DRAWING, DESIGN NAME, DRAWING NO

This community consists of three interrelated technical metadata fields used in drawing documents—NAME OF DRAWING, DESIGN NAME, and DRAWING NO. The entities are connected through explicit relationships indicating that they are paired or co-described title-block identifiers that collectively identify and reference the same drawing sheet. Specifically, NAME OF DRAWING and DRAWING NO are treated as paired identifiers for an official drawing number and corresponding drawing name, while DESIGN NAME is described as a design-level metadata field that is also linked to the drawing reference number (DRAWING NO). No legal, organizational, or reputational claims are provided; the available information focuses purely on documentation/metadata usage within technical drawings.

## NAME OF DRAWING is a title-block metadata field identifying a drawing’s name.

NAME OF DRAWING is described as a technical-document field used to identify the drawing’s name or title, appearing in the document header and title block as the place where the drawing title is recorded [Data: Entities (1557); Relationships (2301, 2951)]. It is associated with labels such as “도면명/Name Of Drawing,” indicating it functions as the designated slot for the sheet’s subject/title within the title block [Data: Entities (1557)].

Within the community, NAME OF DRAWING is directly paired with DRAWING NO to form a combined set of identifiers for the same drawing sheet, supporting clear cross-referencing in documentation [Data: Relationships (2301, 2951)]. This pairing implies that the drawing name is meant to be used alongside the drawing number for unambiguous sheet identification [Data: Relationships (2951)].

## DRAWING NO serves as the drawing number identifier within the title block.

DRAWING NO is defined as a title-block field used on architectural drawings or plan sheets to identify the drawing number for the specific sheet, document, or plan set [Data: Entities (2077)]. It functions as a unique reference identifying the sheet within a broader set of plans [Data: Entities (2077)].

The relationships in this dataset frame DRAWING NO as part of a coordinated identification mechanism: it is explicitly linked to NAME OF DRAWING as a paired title-block identifier, and it is also linked to DESIGN NAME as a metadata reference number that contextualizes the design within the drawing [Data: Relationships (2301, 3236, 2951)]. This suggests the community’s purpose is to ensure that drawing documents can be reliably indexed and retrieved by both name and number [Data: Relationships (2951)].

## DESIGN NAME is a separate design metadata field linked to the drawing reference number.

DESIGN NAME is described as a design name field in the drawing metadata [Data: Entities (2219)]. In the community context, it is connected to DRAWING NO through a relationship that characterizes both as metadata fields describing the design and its drawing reference number [Data: Relationships (3236)].

This implies a two-level structure: DESIGN NAME provides the design-level label, while DRAWING NO provides the reference number tying that design metadata to a particular drawing sheet or plan set [Data: Relationships (3236)]. While the dataset does not describe any additional operational behavior beyond this descriptive linkage, the phrasing “metadata fields” indicates the fields are intended to be used together for document management and identification [Data: Relationships (3236)].

## NAME OF DRAWING and DRAWING NO are paired to identify the same drawing sheet.

The relationship labeled 2301 states that NAME OF DRAWING and DRAWING NO are both title block fields identifying the drawing and its number [Data: Relationships (2301)]. The relationship labeled 2951 further elaborates that DRAWING NO and NAME OF DRAWING are paired title-block identifiers used to identify the same drawing sheet, providing the drawing’s official number and its corresponding name [Data: Relationships (2951)].

This pairing is important because it directly connects two fields that otherwise serve different descriptive functions—one for the sheet’s name/title and one for the sheet’s number—into a single coherent identification pattern for documentation [Data: Relationships (2951)]. The dataset explicitly states that both appear in the title block and together enable the sheet to be clearly identified and referenced in documentation [Data: Relationships (2951)].

## The community emphasizes consistent documentation referencing through structured title-block fields.

Across the relationships, the recurring theme is structured identification: multiple fields are described as title-block or metadata fields that collectively ensure correct referencing. For example, 2301 characterizes NAME OF DRAWING and DRAWING NO as identifying the drawing and its number [Data: Relationships (2301)], while 3236 characterizes DESIGN NAME and DRAWING NO as describing the design and its drawing reference number [Data: Relationships (3236)].

The relationship 2951 summarizes the intent more directly: the paired fields provide an official drawing number and corresponding name to ensure the sheet can be clearly identified and referenced [Data: Relationships (2951)]. This indicates the community’s “capability” is not technical system operation per se, but rather the standardization of metadata used for reliable document indexing and traceability [Data: Entities (1557, 2219, 2077); Relationships (2301, 3236, 2951)].

## Degrees associated with fields are present, but no interpretation is provided in the data.

The entities include a “degree” attribute (e.g., NAME OF DRAWING degree 8, DESIGN NAME degree 2, DRAWING NO degree 2), indicating that each entity has an associated numeric value [Data: Entities (1557, 2219, 2077)]. However, the dataset does not define what these degrees represent (e.g., importance, relevance, frequency, or risk).

Because no legend or explanatory mapping for the “degree” metric is provided, the safest grounded interpretation is that degrees exist as numeric attributes tied to each field description, rather than as a validated measure of operational impact. Any inference beyond “degrees are provided” would not be supported by the text [Data: Entities (1557, 2219, 2077)].

title: AW and Window Schedule Sheets (창 호 도 - 3, 창 호 도 - 4)

full_content:
# AW and Window Schedule Sheets (창 호 도 - 3, 창 호 도 - 4)

This community is centered on the window designation “AW,” which functions as a consistent label in architectural drawing materials. AW is referenced across multiple window schedule sheets—specifically “창 호 도 - 3” (pages 72–73) and “창 호 도 - 4” (pages 73–74)—where it appears in schedule entries describing window types, quantities, locations, and related specifications. A separate relationship indicates that AW is also used alongside “PJ” in the drawing schedule and specifications, suggesting AW participates in broader window-type designation combinations used in the same documentation set. [Data: Entities (1552, 1550, 1551); Relationships (2067, 1982, 1983)].

## AW is a standardized window-type designation used across drawings and schedules

AW is described as a window designation used in drawing and window schedules to identify window units of various types, quantities, and locations, and it serves as a consistent label used throughout the drawings to reference the relevant window configuration [Data: Entities (1552); Relationships (2067)].

Because AW is explicitly framed as a consistent label, it indicates an information structure where designations are reused systematically. This matters for information discovery and downstream interpretation because it implies that any analysis of window components must treat “AW” as the authoritative category label in the schedule rather than a one-off description. [Data: Entities (1552)].

## AW window schedule content is maintained on two related sheets: 창 호 도 - 3 and 창 호 도 - 4

The entity “창 호 도 - 3” is a window schedule drawing sheet referenced on pages 72–73, containing window symbols, types, sizes, locations, finishes, hardware, and glass specifications for multiple AW windows and associated notes [Data: Entities (1550); Relationships (1982)].

Additionally, “창 호 도 - 4” continues this window schedule on pages 73–74, listing additional AW windows, their locations, and quantities [Data: Entities (1551); Relationships (1983)]. This split across two sequential sheets suggests the documentation is designed to be read as a set, where completeness for AW entries likely requires checking both sheets rather than only one. [Data: Entities (1550, 1551); Relationships (1982, 1983)].

## 창 호 도 - 3 contains AW schedule entries and specifications

The relationship data explicitly states that “창 호 도 - 3 contains the schedule entries for AW window types and their specifications” [Data: Relationships (1982)].

This means AW’s descriptive and technical attributes (such as types and specifications, and as described in the sheet summary, potentially finishes/hardware/glass) are not merely referenced indirectly; they are directly enumerated within 창 호 도 - 3. For analysts, this sheet is a primary location to extract AW-related configuration details and to understand how AW is defined within the project’s window schedule conventions. [Data: Entities (1550); Relationships (1982)].

## 창 호 도 - 4 contains additional AW schedule entries including quantities and locations

The relationship data explicitly states that “창 호 도 - 4 also contains schedule entries for AW window types and quantities” [Data: Relationships (1983)].

Given the entity description that it lists additional AW windows with locations and quantities, this sheet likely complements 창 호 도 - 3 by expanding coverage of AW beyond the initial page range. For information completeness, any determination of total counts or distribution of AW windows should incorporate entries from both sheets. [Data: Entities (1551); Relationships (1983)].

## AW appears in conjunction with PJ, indicating combined window-type designation usage

A relationship indicates that “AW and PJ are window-type designations appearing together in the drawing schedule and specifications” [Data: Relationships (2067)].

This suggests the documentation system supports either combined forms or co-referenced designation sets, where AW is used alongside PJ within the same scheduling context. Practically, this means interpretation of AW may require awareness that it is sometimes paired with PJ; analysts should look for schedule rows or notes where both designations are present to correctly understand the intended window configuration and how the two designations interact. [Data: Relationships (2067)].

title: Window & Curtain-Wall Specification Community (창 호 도 -4, 창 호 도 -5, PJ)
summary: This community is centered on two architectural drawing/schedule sheets—창 호 도 -4 and 창 호 도 -5—that define window and glazing requirements for specific parts of a building. The sheets reference consistent component families (a projectable window type designated PJ, an aluminum insulated curtain-wall/window assembly sized 160x60, accessory hardware requirements, and a roll insect screen) and specify low-E double glazing glass thicknesses (THK 24 and THK 26). Additional allowance notes on 창 호 도 -4 further constrain door handle hardware style options (lever-type, or horizontal/vertical bar-type). Overall, the entities are tightly related through explicit specification relationships, forming a structured set of constraints for fenestration design and installation rather than a socio-political or adversarial actor network.
full_content:
# Window & Curtain-Wall Specification Community (창 호 도 -4, 창 호 도 -5, PJ)

This community is centered on two architectural drawing/schedule sheets—창 호 도 -4 and 창 호 도 -5—that define window and glazing requirements for specific parts of a building. The sheets reference consistent component families (a projectable window type designated PJ, an aluminum insulated curtain-wall/window assembly sized 160x60, accessory hardware requirements, and a roll insect screen) and specify low-E double glazing glass thicknesses (THK 24 and THK 26). Additional allowance notes on 창 호 도 -4 further constrain door handle hardware style options (lever-type, or horizontal/vertical bar-type). Overall, the entities are tightly related through explicit specification relationships, forming a structured set of constraints for fenestration design and installation rather than a socio-political or adversarial actor network.

## 창 호 도 -4 is a primary specification sheet labeled A that bundles window type, glazing, and hardware requirements.

The sheet 창 호 도 -4 is explicitly tied to section/sheet marker A, indicating it is part of a structured drawing set. [Data: Entities (1559); Relationships (2001)].

Within 창 호 도 -4, the glazing specification includes the projectable window type designation PJ, meaning the sheet’s requirements are not limited to glass but also define compatible window configuration. [Data: Entities (1561); Relationships (2039)].

창 호 도 -4 also specifies the aluminum insulated curtain-wall and project window assembly (160 x 60), connecting the sheet to a particular frame/assembly size and type. [Data: Entities (1566); Relationships (2041)].

In addition, the sheet enumerates accessory hardware inclusion (“기타부속철물일체”) and requires that roll insect screens be included (“롤방충망 포함”), which indicates the specification governs not only performance glazing but also integrated functional components. [Data: Entities (1563, 1564); Relationships (2048, 2050)].

Finally, 창 호 도 -4 defines a drawing scale of 1:100, which reinforces that the sheet is a detailed design document intended for accurate component placement and dimensional coordination. [Data: Entities (1558); Relationships (2003)].

## PJ and THK 24 low-E double glazing are directly linked through the 창 호 도 -4 glazing specification.

The project window type designation PJ appears in the 창 호 도 -4 sheet as part of its glazing specification, establishing that the glazing requirements are tied to a specific window behavior/configuration (projectable/projecting). [Data: Entities (1561); Relationships (2039)].

The same sheet specifies 24 mm thick low-E double glazing for project window portions (THK 24 로이 복층유리), making THK 24 a direct glass-performance constraint for the window type referenced on the sheet. [Data: Entities (1569); Relationships (2045)].

Because the data describes THK 24 로이 복층유리 as a low-E double glazing system intended to work with an insulated aluminum window frame (and as specified as an insulated glass assembly), the linkage between PJ and THK 24 suggests that correct pairing of window configuration and glass assembly is essential to meet the intended insulation/energy-efficiency goals described in the entity description. [Data: Entities (1569, 1561); Relationships (2039, 2045)].

## 창 호 도 -5 is another labeled specification sheet that ties glazing/frames to stairwell usage.

The drawing/schedule sheet 창 호 도 -5 is labeled with section/sheet marker A, showing it belongs to the same organized documentation set as 창 호 도 -4. [Data: Entities (1560); Relationships (2002)].

Unlike 창 호 도 -4, the explicit downstream linkage shown for 창 호 도 -5 is to the aluminum insulated curtain-wall window assembly sized 160x60 used in the stairwell location (“160X60 알루미늄 단열커튼월창”). This indicates that 창 호 도 -5 includes specific fenestration requirements for a particular building area (stairs). [Data: Entities (1567); Relationships (2043)].

## THK 26 low-E double glazing appears as a separate glazing detail with a stated layer build-up.

A glazing detail entity for THK 26 로이 복층유리 is present and is linked to a specific glazing layer build-up string (“6LE, PLAONE +14AR(SWS)+6CL”). This directly anchors THK 26 to both performance type (low-E) and a defined internal construction. [Data: Entities (1568); Relationships (2267)].

However, in the provided relationship data, no explicit connection is shown between 창 호 도 -4 or 창 호 도 -5 and THK 26. As a result, THK 26 can be described confidently as a glazing detail that exists within the community, but its specific placement within the two sheets cannot be concluded from the given records alone. [Data: Entities (1568); Relationships (2267)].

## The community includes functional and integration requirements: accessory hardware and roll insect screens are mandated on 창 호 도 -4.

The specification entity “기타부속철물일체” is included as part of the 창 호 도 -4 sheet requirements, explicitly stating that all other accessory hardware is included as part of the window assembly. This means the sheet is not limited to major components (frames and glass) but also includes completeness expectations for the assembly package. [Data: Entities (1563); Relationships (2048)].

Additionally, “롤방충망 포함” is required by 창 호 도 -4, indicating the inclusion of a roll insect screen as part of the specification. This is an important integration detail because it can affect operational clearances, packaging, and installation sequencing. [Data: Entities (1564); Relationships (2050)].

## 창 호 도 -4 contains specific door-hardware allowance notes (lever and bar-style handle types) that constrain acceptable hardware.

Beyond window/glazing, the notes on 창 호 도 -4 allow lever-type handles (레버형). This suggests that the drawing’s notes govern broader openings/hardware compatibility rules, not only glazing assemblies. [Data: Entities (1572); Relationships (2055)].

The sheet also allows horizontal or vertical bar-style door handle types (수평 또는 수직막대형), providing explicit alternative handle styles that meet the note instructions. [Data: Entities (1573); Relationships (2056)].

Because these are “allowance” relationships rather than a single required handle, they likely function as constraints for selecting hardware that fits an approved set of design standards captured in the drawing set. [Data: Entities (1572, 1573); Relationships (2055, 2056)].

title: Project Level Reference Community: 지하층 바닥, 1층/2층/3층 바닥, G.L
summary: This community is defined by construction drawing “level references” that establish vertical datums across architectural plans and sections. It centers on the ground level marker (G.L) and a chain of floor/slab levels—지하층 바닥 → 1층 바닥 → 2층 바닥 → 3층 바닥—where each level is represented consistently across elevations, sections, longitudinal sections, and drawing schedules/key plans. full_content:
# Project Level Reference Community: 지하층 바닥, 1층/2층/3층 바닥, G.L

This community is defined by construction drawing “level references” that establish vertical datums across architectural plans and sections. It centers on the ground level marker (G.L) and a chain of floor/slab levels—지하층 바닥 → 1층 바닥 → 2층 바닥 → 3층 바닥—where each level is represented consistently across elevations, sections, longitudinal sections, and drawing schedules/key plans. The relationships also connect these level markers to specific elevation annotations (e.g., EL+28.6) and to an external elevation drawing context (107동 right-side elevation), indicating how the same vertical reference framework is reused to interpret multiple views.

## A vertical level chain links basement to ground and upper floors consistently across drawings.

The community’s core structure is a sequential hierarchy of floor/slab levels: 지하층 바닥 (basement), 1층 바닥 (first floor), 2층 바닥 (second floor), and 3층 바닥 (third floor). The relationships explicitly describe that each higher slab level is shown above the level immediately below, forming a coherent vertical ordering used for interpretation of the architectural documentation [Data: Entities (1346, 1294, 1295, 1296); Relationships (1609, 1608, 1607)].

This chain matters because the definitions of each level reference describe consistent use across multiple drawing types (elevation, section, longitudinal section, key plan, drawing schedule), suggesting the project relies on these markers as the standardized “datum” for locating building elements vertically. In practice, if multiple drawings agree on the same level reference, it reduces ambiguity when matching geometry between plan and section views [Data: Entities (1294, 1295, 1296, 1346); Relationships (1607, 1608, 1609)].

## G.L functions as the ground/grade datum and is used as the reference line for elevation measurements.

G.L is defined as the ground level reference (vertical datum) used in architectural drawings, including layout drawings, elevation drawings, and section drawings. The documentation describes G.L as a “reference line from which elevation measurements are indicated,” with examples of G.L elevation values (e.g., G.L (+19.60), G.L (+20.40)) [Data: Entities (229)].

Additionally, a relationship shows G.L associated with a specific elevation context: ground level markers are shown in the right-side elevation drawing for “107동.” This indicates G.L is not only a abstract datum but is actively rendered in elevation views to anchor how heights and elevations are read across the project [Data: Relationships (241)].

## Upper floor slab levels (1층/2층/3층 바닥) are treated as consistent benchmarks across elevations, sections, and schedules.

The definitions for 1층 바닥, 2층 바닥, and 3층 바닥 emphasize that each is shown consistently as a floor level reference in multiple drawing categories, including elevation drawings, section drawings, longitudinal section drawings, and also listed in the key plan and drawing schedule. This shows that the project uses these level references not only in graphical views but also in project documentation management (key plan/schedules) to ensure consistent reference usage [Data: Entities (1294, 1295, 1296)].

The relationships further confirm the intended vertical ordering among these benchmarks: 2층 바닥 is shown above 1층 바닥, and 3층 바닥 is shown above 2층 바닥. Together, the textual definitions and relational constraints indicate these levels serve as a repeatable coordinate framework for interpreting building geometry across views [Data: Relationships (1608, 1607)].

## Basement floor level is linked to a specific elevation marker (EL+28.6) in the key plan.

The basement level reference, 지하층 바닥, is associated with a specific elevation marker “EL+28.6” in the key plan. This relationship indicates that the basement level is not merely labeled as a generic basement floor; it is tied to an explicit elevation value used for reference in documentation [Data: Relationships (1652); Entities (1346)].

This linkage is important for traceability: when different drawings (e.g., sections, key plans) reference the same numeric elevation for the basement, teams can align vertical datum interpretations and reduce errors in reading where basement elements sit relative to other level markers [Data: Relationships (1652); Entities (1346)].

## The documentation shows cross-view consistency for 지하층 바닥 (basement) across sections and longitudinal sections.

The definition of 지하층 바닥 states that it appears in sections (“단면도”) and is also shown in longitudinal sections (“종단면도”). It further notes that the basement floor level is recorded in the key plan and section/elevation-related drawings (키 플랜과 단면 입면도), demonstrating consistent reference usage across multiple representation types [Data: Entities (1346)].

Because this basement level reference repeats across these views, it functions as a stable anchor for matching features that appear in sectioned representations but may be hard to correlate using only one view type. The relationship to EL+28.6 reinforces that this anchor also carries numeric meaning in at least one key documentation artifact (the key plan) [Data: Entities (1346); Relationships (1652)].

## Each level reference is likely intended for interoperability between drawings via shared naming and datum conventions.

Across the entity definitions, the pattern is consistent: each level reference is described as being shown across multiple drawing types and also incorporated into key plans and/or drawing schedules. For instance, 1층 바닥 and 2층 바닥 are described as appearing across elevation drawings, section drawings, and longitudinal section drawings, and both are also listed in key plan and drawing schedule [Data: Entities (1294, 1295)].

This suggests the community is less about programmatic “actors” and more about a systematic documentation network: naming conventions and datum definitions enable interoperability between different drawing outputs. The relationships describing relative vertical placement (above/below) further formalize these conventions into a hierarchy that can be used to interpret the building’s vertical structure consistently [Data: Relationships (1607, 1608, 1609)].

title: Building Level-Reference Community: EL+39.7, Floor Levels, and 정면도 Firefighter Access
summary: This community is a set of construction-documentation entities focused on how a building’s vertical levels (e.g., 3rd/4th/5th floor and roof-related floors) are defined and cross-referenced across architectural drawings. The relationships connect level markers and floor labels to specific drawing types (e.g., section A-A and 정면도) and to each other in a consistent stacking order (3층 바닥 < 4층 바닥 < 5층 바닥 < 옥상층 바닥), with additional emphasis in the 정면도 on an emergency component: a firefighter access window detail. A rooftop/roof-layer level (옥상층 바닥 and 지붕층 바닥) is also used as a coordination reference in system-related diagram context (e.g., piping/installation planning for a solar thermal system), indicating that the level-marking conventions extend beyond pure architectural layout into MEP coordination.
full_content:
# Building Level-Reference Community: EL+39.7, Floor Levels, and 정면도 Firefighter Access

This community is a set of construction-documentation entities focused on how a building’s vertical levels (e.g., 3rd/4th/5th floor and roof-related floors) are defined and cross-referenced across architectural drawings. The relationships connect level markers and floor labels to specific drawing types (e.g., section A-A and 정면도) and to each other in a consistent stacking order (3층 바닥 < 4층 바닥 < 5층 바닥 < 옥상층 바닥), with additional emphasis in the 정면도 on an emergency component: a firefighter access window detail. A rooftop/roof-layer level (옥상층 바닥 and 지붕층 바닥) is also used as a coordination reference in system-related diagram context (e.g., piping/installation planning for a solar thermal system), indicating that the level-marking conventions extend beyond pure architectural layout into MEP coordination.

## EL+39.7 functions as an elevation-marker annotation tied to section drawing context.

EL+39.7 is described as an elevation level marker used in section and elevation drawings to indicate a specific vertical height within the building, functioning as a reference point for vertical positioning in the documentation set. The text further indicates that it is associated with floor level markings (including references to third- and fourth-floor levels), implying its role as a stable annotation even if the exact floor designation differs across the described sources. [Data: Entities (1310); Relationships (1684)]

The relationship explicitly connects EL+39.7 to “SECTION A-A,” stating that EL+39.7 appears as one of the elevation markers shown in that section view. This means EL+39.7 is not only a standalone label; it is anchored to a particular drawing sheet/view, strengthening interpretability and reducing ambiguity when translating between section/elevation representations. [Data: Relationships (1684)]

## Floor-level entities (3층 바닥→4층 바닥→5층 바닥→옥상층 바닥) are presented as an ordered vertical stack across drawings.

The community includes multiple explicit floor-level reference entities: 4층 바닥 (fourth-floor slab level), 5층 바닥 (fifth-floor slab level), and 옥상층 바닥 (rooftop floor level). Each is described as a consistent reference used across elevation drawings and section drawings, and each is also listed within key plan and schedule contexts, indicating cross-document coordination. [Data: Entities (1297,1298,1299); Relationships (1605,1606,1604)]

Crucially, relationships define the vertical ordering between these levels in a consistent direction: 5층 바닥 is above 4층 바닥, and 4층 바닥 is above 3층 바닥; additionally, the roof-floor level is shown above the fifth-floor slab level. This ordered set suggests a documentation convention where floor elevations can be mapped reliably from lower to upper levels when analyzing building structure and drawing cross-references. [Data: Relationships (1605,1606,1604)]

## 옥상층 바닥 is used as a unifying rooftop coordination level, including for solar thermal piping/installation diagrams.

옥상층 바닥 is described not only as a rooftop floor level reference used across project section/elevation drawings and schedules, but also as a coordination point used in the piping and installation diagram for the solar thermal system. This indicates that the rooftop elevation labeling serves both architectural referencing and systems coordination (i.e., MEP-related planning and layout constraints tied to the rooftop level). [Data: Entities (1299); Relationships (1604,1606)]

Because the description explicitly states that 옥상층 바닥 is referenced across the project’s section/elevation/key plan/schedules and then again within system diagrams, it implies that downstream design artifacts rely on the accuracy and consistency of this rooftop level marker. The risk/impact associated with such a marker typically stems from potential miscoordination if the rooftop elevation is misunderstood between disciplines (architecture vs. system installation). [Data: Entities (1299)]

## 지붕층 바닥 and 옥상층 바닥 are treated as related roof-level designations but appear as distinct entities in the documentation.

The community includes two roof-related level reference entities: 지붕층 바닥 and 옥상층 바닥. 지붕층 바닥 is described as a roof floor level reference used across project drawings, shown in section and elevation drawings, and included in schedule/key plan/floor elevation notes. [Data: Entities (1300)]

A relationship states that 지붕층 바닥 and 옥상층 바닥 are both roof-related floor levels marked in the section/elevation drawings, indicating they are semantically linked as roof-level markers while still being represented as distinct labels/entities. This duality suggests a documentation practice where “roof-layer” and “rooftop floor” may correspond to different conceptual elevations or layers (even if both are roof-related), requiring careful interpretation when consolidating elevation data. [Data: Relationships (1603); Entities (1300,1299)]

## 정면도 (elevation drawing) includes firefighter access window detail, creating an explicit emergency-access linkage in the drawing set.

정면도 is described as a front elevation view referenced within the architectural drawing set, showing the building’s facade and including details such as a fire fighter access window and related specifics. The description emphasizes that the drawing supports verification not only of exterior form but also of evacuation and firefighting-related elements. [Data: Entities (233)]

A relationship further specifies that 정면도 includes a “소방관 진입창문” (firefighter access window) detail, describing it as designed to support firefighter access and entry during fire or other emergency situations. This indicates that within the community, the emergency-access component is directly embedded in the elevation drawing artifacts rather than being only mentioned in text schedules or separate narrative documentation. [Data: Relationships (1514)]

## The drawing-scale specification (축척: 1/200) connects to 정면도 and standardizes interpretation of the elevation drawings.

축척 : 1/200 is described as a drawing scale specification shown for A1/A3 on the elevation drawings. A relationship connects the scale specification to 정면도, stating that the elevation drawings show an A1/A3 scale of 1/200. [Data: Entities (238); Relationships (249)]

While scale itself does not define structural function, it affects measurement fidelity when analysts extract or compare geometric dimensions from the elevation drawings. In a community centered on level markers and coordination across views, a consistent scale specification supports correct interpretation of the relative positions and heights implied by the floor-level entities and emergency-access details shown in the elevations. [Data: Entities (238); Relationships (249,1514)]

## Cross-section anchoring via “횡 단 면 도 -3” and KEY PLAN suggests navigational orientation of the section within the building layout.

“횡 단 면 도 -3” (Cross Section Drawing -3) is described as a technical construction drawing containing architectural section details, level markers, material layers, and notes for a building design. It is related to KEY PLAN through a relationship stating that the section drawing includes a key plan reference to orient the section within the building layout. [Data: Entities (1453); Relationships (1860)]

This implies that the community’s level markers and section-level information are not isolated—they are systematically tied to an overall layout orientation tool (KEY PLAN). Such linkage improves analysts’ ability to relate section cuts to spatial context when interpreting the vertical references (e.g., roof vs. floor slab levels) and any associated construction notes contained within 횡 단 면 도 -3. [Data: Relationships (1860)]

full_content:
# Elevation Drawings and Facade Materials: 1/150 Scale, 영롱쌓기, and 콘크리트블록

This community centers on a set of architectural elevation drawing sheets and their linked facade design specifications. The drawing set includes multiple elevation views (including front/정면도, left side/좌측면도, and rear/배면도) that share boundary and floor-level context, and it repeatedly documents the facade’s masonry system: 콘크리트블록 arranged using the openwork stacking pattern 영롱쌓기 (purposefully leaving gaps/openings for a decorative, functional screen-like effect). Relationships within the community connect the elevation sheets to the specified scale (1/150), to the named pattern 영롱쌓기 on the front facade, and to the mapping of 콘크리트블록 to that same openwork pattern on the facade.

## The facade design community is anchored by 콘크리트블록 specified with the openwork stacking pattern 영롱쌓기 (purposefully creating gaps/openings).

콘크리트블록 is explicitly described as an exterior masonry finishing material used on building facade elevations, and within this community it is tied to a specific arrangement approach: 영롱쌓기. 영롱쌓기는 openwork/perforated stacking applied to concrete blocks, described as intentionally laid masonry with gaps or openings to produce a decorative and functional screen-like effect.

This linkage matters because it shows the design intent is not just “concrete block,” but concrete block placed in a particular perforated/openwork configuration on the facade. The relationships also directly state that 콘크리트블록 is specified with 영롱쌓기 on the facade, indicating that the pattern is an integrated part of the facade construction/finishing specification rather than an incidental note. [Data: Entities (1312, 1313); Relationships (1579)]

## 정면도 (front elevation) repeatedly documents the drawing scale as 1/150 and annotates the facade pattern 영롱쌓기.

The community includes a front elevation drawing labeled 정면도 that is associated with the architectural drawing scale repeatedly shown as 1/150. The relationship record states that “The front elevation drawing uses a 1/150 scale,” tying the scale entity (1/150) directly to 정면도.

In addition to scale, the same front elevation drawing notes the use of the openwork stacking pattern 영롱쌓기. Together, these two links indicate that the front facade is specified at a particular drawing scale and that the openwork masonry pattern is a named and documented feature in the front view documentation. [Data: Entities (1316); Relationships (1557, 1527)]

## The elevation drawings in the community (좌측면도 and 배면도) are connected by shared building context and floor-level references.

좌측면도 (left side elevation) and 배면도 (rear elevation) are both elevation drawing sheets of the same building, and they share the same floor-level references and boundary context. This is grounded in the relationship stating that both are elevation drawings of the same building and share floor-level references and boundary context.

The presence of shared references suggests the community’s technical documentation is coordinated across multiple views, enabling consistent interpretation of where facade finishes (including the concrete block system) apply relative to boundaries and floors. While only the relation between the two elevation sheets is explicitly supported here, the shared context strengthens the reliability of using one view’s information to interpret another’s. [Data: Entities (1289, 1290); Relationships (1515)]

## 배면도 (rear elevation) is presented in A1 format, indicating a formal, standard drawing sheet setting within the community’s documentation.

The rear elevation drawing 배면도 is linked to an A1 format presentation via a relationship record. This implies the drawing is produced/presented on a large standardized sheet size, which is typical for detailed elevation deliverables.

Within this community context, the A1 presentation does not itself establish facade performance or risk, but it does reinforce that these elevation drawings are part of a structured and formal architectural documentation set—consistent with repeat, explicit specification of facade materials/patterns across views. [Data: Entities (1290); Relationships (1522)]

## 영롱쌓기 is defined as a decorative and functional openwork/perforated stacking method for concrete block facades, connecting material definition to facade intent.

The entity 영롱쌓기 is described in detail as an architectural masonry pattern for facade design. It is characterized as an openwork or perforated stacking method applied to concrete blocks, intentionally laid with gaps/openings to create a decorative and functional screen-like effect.

This definitional linkage matters because it connects technical drawing annotations to a functional design rationale: the facade is intended to be visually permeable and provides a screen-like quality through the masonry gaps/openings. In the community, this intent is tied back to the named material system (콘크리트블록) and the facade specification relationship. [Data: Entities (1313, 1312); Relationships (1579)]

## The community’s relationships indicate a multi-view documentation approach: scale and pattern notes appear at least in the front view, while other views maintain shared reference context.

The documented relationships show that the front elevation (정면도) has both scale (1/150) and pattern (영롱쌓기) annotations linked to it. Meanwhile, left and rear elevations (좌측면도 and 배면도) are connected through shared floor-level and boundary context.

Taken together, the community appears to represent a cohesive set of elevation drawings where at least one view (front) explicitly documents the facade’s openwork masonry pattern and drawing scale, and other views (left/rear) support consistent interpretation through shared referencing. This structure helps decision-makers understand that the facade system is part of a coordinated set of deliverables rather than an isolated note. [Data: Relationships (1557, 1527, 1515)]
